content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN.

You are asked to find total number of integer pairs (A,B)(A,B)(A,B) such that


1≤A,B≤N 1 \leq A,B \leq N1≤A,B≤N
A2+B2+gcd2(A,B)+lcm2(A,B)=NA^2+B^2+gcd^2(A,B)+lcm^2(A,B)=NA2+B2+gcd2(A,B)+lcm2(A,B)=N. Note that gcd2(A,B)gcd^2(A, B)gcd2(A,B) and lcm2(A,B)lcm^2(A, B)lcm2(A,B) denote the square of gcd and the square of lcm of numbers AAA and BBB respectively.

Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The only line of each test case contains an integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line, number of valid pairs (A,B)(A,B)(A,B).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^10
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (4 ≤ N ≤ 10^10).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of ordered pairs (A, B) (1 ≤ A, B ≤ N) that satisfy A² + B² + gcd(A, B)² + lcm(A, B)² = N.

solution.approach:
Rewrite the condition using g = gcd(A, B) and a = A/g, b = B/g (so gcd(a, b) = 1). Then lcm(A, B) = g·a·b and the equation becomes:
    g²·(a² + b² + 1 + a²·b²) = N.
Let S = a² + b² + 1 + a²·b². For a fixed coprime pair (a, b) the value S is unique. The pair (A, B) is valid iff there exists an integer g such that g² = N / S (i.e., N is divisible by S and the quotient is a perfect square). Therefore:
1. Pre‑compute for all coprime (a, b) with a, b ≥ 1 the value S ≤ 10¹⁰ and store for each S the number of ordered pairs contributed by this (a, b) – 2 if a ≠ b, 1 if a = b.
2. For each test case factor N, generate all square divisors g², compute S = N / g² and add the pre‑computed contribution of S (if any).
The pre‑computation iterates a from 1 up to √MAX_N (≈10⁵) and for each a iterates b up to √MAX_N / a, stopping when S exceeds the limit. The total number of (a, b) examined is about 1.2·10⁶, easily fast enough.
Factorisation of N uses a list of primes up to 10⁵ (sieve). Generating square divisors is done by recursion over the prime exponents, using only the floor(e/2) possibilities for each prime.
The answer for a test case is the sum of contributions of all S = N / g².
Time complexity: pre‑computation O(√MAX_N·log √MAX_N) ≈ 1.2·10⁶ operations. For each test case, factorisation O(√N / log √N) ≤ 10⁴ and enumeration of square divisors is at most a few hundred, so overall O(T·√N) in the worst case, well within limits. Space complexity: O(number of distinct S) ≈ 1.2·10⁶ entries (≈ 10 MB).
solution.timeComplexity: Pre‑computation: O(1.2·10⁶). Per test case: O(√N) for factorisation + O(number of square divisors) ≤ O(10⁴). Overall ≤ O(T·10⁴) in the worst case, but sum of N ≤ 10¹⁰ keeps it fast.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1.2·10⁶) for the map of S → contribution (≈ 10 MB) plus O(π(10⁵)) for the prime list.
